section: Section 1 - Short title, extent, application and commencement
content: This Ordinance may be called the Muslim Family Laws Ordinance, 1961. It extends to the whole of Pakistan and applies to all Muslim citizens of Pakistan, wherever they may be.

section: Section 3 - Ordinance to override other laws
content: The provisions of this Ordinance have effect notwithstanding any law, custom or usage, and the registration of Muslim marriages takes place only in accordance with these provisions. The Arbitration Act 1940, the Code of Civil Procedure 1908, and any other law regulating the procedure of Courts do not apply to any Arbitration Council.

In the event of the death of any son or daughter of the propositus before the opening of succession, the children of such son or daughter, if any, living at the time the succession opens, shall per stripes receive a share equivalent to the share which such son or daughter, as the case may be, would have received if alive.

Every marriage solemnized under Muslim Law shall be registered in accordance with the provisions of this Ordinance. The Union Council grants licences to Nikah Registrars, and no more than one Nikah Registrar can be licensed for any one Ward. Any marriage not solemnized by the Nikah Registrar must be reported to him by the person who solemnized it. Contravention is punishable with simple imprisonment up to three months, or a fine up to one thousand rupees, or both.

No man, during the subsistence of an existing marriage, may contract another marriage without the previous written permission of the Arbitration Council, and no such marriage contracted without permission shall be registered. An application for permission must be submitted to the Chairman, stating reasons for the proposed marriage and whether the consent of the existing wife or wives has been obtained. The Arbitration Council may grant permission if satisfied the proposed marriage is necessary and just. A husband who contracts another marriage without permission must immediately pay the entire amount of dower (prompt or deferred) due to the existing wife or wives, recoverable as arrears of land revenue, and on conviction is punishable with simple imprisonment up to one year, or a fine up to five thousand rupees, or both.

Any man who wishes to divorce his wife shall, as soon as possible after pronouncing talaq in any form, give the Chairman written notice of having done so, and supply a copy to the wife. Contravention is punishable with simple imprisonment up to one year, or a fine up to five thousand rupees, or both. Talaq is not effective until the expiration of ninety days from the day notice is delivered to the Chairman. Within thirty days of receiving notice, the Chairman constitutes an Arbitration Council to attempt reconciliation between the parties. If the wife is pregnant at the time talaq is pronounced, talaq does not take effect until the ninety-day period or the end of the pregnancy, whichever is later. A wife whose marriage was terminated by an effective talaq may remarry the same husband without an intervening marriage to a third person, unless this is the third such termination.

section: Section 8 - Dissolution of marriage otherwise than by talaq
content: Where the right to divorce has been duly delegated to the wife and she wishes to exercise it, or where either party wishes to dissolve the marriage otherwise than by talaq, the notice and reconciliation procedure under Section 7 applies in the same way.

If a husband fails to maintain his wife adequately, or fails to maintain multiple wives equitably, the wife or wives may apply to the Chairman, who constitutes an Arbitration Council to determine the matter. The Arbitration Council may issue a certificate specifying the maintenance amount payable by the husband. Either spouse may apply for revision of the certificate to the Collector, whose decision is final and cannot be questioned in any Court. Unpaid amounts are recoverable as arrears of land revenue.
Where no details about the mode of payment of dower are specified in the nikahnama or marriage contract, the entire amount of the dower is presumed to be payable on demand.

section: Section 13 - Amendment relating to Dissolution of Muslim Marriages Act 1939
content: In the Dissolution of Muslim Marriages Act 1939, a new ground (ii-a) was inserted allowing a wife to seek dissolution of marriage where the husband has taken an additional wife in contravention of the Muslim Family Laws Ordinance 1961's polygamy provisions.
